title: Model-Native Code Truncation
concept: Why code models cut off or output placeholders (like '// TODO'), and prompt strategies to force completeness.

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of providing examples when prompting coding models?

options.text: To help the model understand the code format

exercises.explain: Providing examples helps the model capture the correct format and style, making it easier to generate accurate code.

cards.title: What is Code Truncation?
cards.body: Code truncation happens when models stop generating code before completing it. This can lead to unfinished snippets, such as '// TODO'.

cards.title: Understanding Model-Native Code Truncation
cards.body: Models may truncate code due to limits in their response length or uncertainty about the next steps they should take.

cards.title: Think of It Like a Recipe
cards.body: When you give a recipe, sometimes the instructions are unclear, leading to half-finished dishes—similar to how models may leave code incomplete.

cards.title: Example of Code Truncation
cards.body: If you ask a model to create a function, it might return: 'function example() { // TODO: implement logic here }'. The '// TODO' indicates unfinished work.

cards.title: Myth: Models Always Finish Code
cards.body: It's a common belief that AI always generates complete code. In reality, they can truncate if they feel uncertain or reach their limit.

cards.title: Completeness vs. Truncation
cards.body: Models aim to generate full code but sometimes cut it off based on their understanding and instruction length limits.

exercises.prompt: Why might AI models leave code unfinished?

options.text: They have too much information to process.
They reach limits on response length or feel uncertain.
They are designed only for short tasks.

exercises.explain: Models can truncate when they hit response limits or aren't confident about what to generate next.

exercises.prompt: True or false: AI models always generate complete and perfect code every time.
exercises.explain: The statement is false because models can truncate code if they reach a limit or if the instructions are unclear.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: When a model generates code but leaves it unfinished, we call it ____.
exercises.sentence: When a model generates code but leaves it unfinished, we call it truncation.
exercises.explain: Truncation is the correct term that describes when the code stops before completion.

exercises.prompt: Which statement is most likely AI-generated?

options.text: Complete your task with these steps.

exercises.explain: The third option is likely AI-generated as it indicates truncation with '// TODO' marking unfinished code.

exercises.prompt: What strategy can help ensure models generate complete code instead of truncating it?

options.text: Use vague prompts that don't limit the information.
Ask the model to generate a long response.

exercises.explain: Providing specific examples helps clarify what you want, reducing the chances of truncation and increasing the likelihood of completeness.
